pattern: A valid pair of cuts exists exactly when merging every rectangle's interval along one axis collapses into three or more separate blocks with a gap between them.
hints: Think about only one axis at a time: if you can make the two cuts horizontally, only the y-ranges of the rectangles matter, and the x-ranges are irrelevant to that choice.

Sort rectangles by where their interval starts along that axis and merge any that overlap into one continuous block; a rectangle whose interval starts at or after the current block's end can never be split from it by a single cut, so it starts a brand new block instead.

Once you know how many separate blocks the merged intervals form along an axis, you can always slide a cut into every gap between two blocks -- so that axis works whenever merging produces at least three blocks; check both axes independently and answer true if either one qualifies.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, no valid pair of cuts
minimal n with three full-width horizontal stripes
three rectangles arranged so no straight cut can isolate them
rectangles exactly touching at the candidate cut lines
one large rectangle blocks any horizontal split while two small ones would allow a vertical one
huge grid dimension with only three rectangles

stress.description: randomized guillotine partition of an n x n grid into many non-overlapping rectangles
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(3, 1000)
    rects = [[0, 0, n, n]]
    target = min(3 + (size % 5000), 20000)
    attempts = 0
    while len(rects) < target and attempts < target * 5:
        attempts += 1
        idx = rng.randrange(len(rects))
        x1, y1, x2, y2 = rects[idx]
        vertical = rng.random() < 0.5
        if vertical and x2 - x1 >= 2:
            cut = rng.randint(x1 + 1, x2 - 1)
            rects[idx] = [x1, y1, cut, y2]
            rects.append([cut, y1, x2, y2])
        elif not vertical and y2 - y1 >= 2:
            cut = rng.randint(y1 + 1, y2 - 1)
            rects[idx] = [x1, y1, x2, cut]
            rects.append([x1, cut, x2, y2])
    return [n, rects]
stress.note: each split is a straight guillotine cut of an existing rectangle, so the pieces never overlap by construction
